authors.creator: Qiao Zixin
authors.life: A Roman general, politician, and engineer. Frontinus was known to us first as the praetor urbanus in AD 70, and he was selected as consul three times, he also governed Britain, consolidating Roman control before being succeeded by Agricola. Renowned for his military expertise, he authored Strategemata, a tactical guide for commanders. As Rome’s water commissioner under Emperor Nerva, he reformed the city’s aqueduct system, and then documented in De Aquaeductu Urbis Romae—a work on hydraulic engineering and urban water supply management. Frontinus emphasized practicality, ethics, and public welfare, blending technical precision with administrative rigor. His works remain vital sources on Roman military science and urban management.

authors.creator: Qiao Zixin
authors.life: Rome’s first historian, belonged to the patrician clan of Fabius, he is a relative of Fabius Maximus “Cunctator”. Fabius Pictor fought in the Gallic War in 225 BC and commanded forces against the Ligurians. As a senator, he led a critical embassy to Delphi after Rome’s defeat at Cannae. Though unconfirmed as a magistrate, his senior status and Greek proficiency suited diplomatic roles. He authored Rome’s history in Greek, blending myth, institutions, and contemporary events like the Second Punic War. Fragments suggest ritual interests, but no direct priestly role is attested. His lineage traced to C. Fabius “Pictor”, and descendants included a praetor. His work, foundational for Roman historiography, aimed to legitimize Rome’s rise to Greek audiences, influencing later historians like Livy.

authors.creator: Qiao Zixin
authors.life: Roman historian and senator active during the Second Punic War. He served as praetor in 210 BC, he governed Sicily under M. Valerius Laevinus until 208 BC, when he attempted to besiege Locri but was defeated by Hannibal. Captured during this campaign, he reportedly conversed with Hannibal in Greek, later incorporating these insights into his history. His work, written in Greek, mirrored Fabius Pictor’s structure, spanning Rome’s foundation to Hannibal’s invasion. However, his narrative likely remained incomplete, possibly due to his death or captivity. Despite offering unique Carthaginian perspectives, his history was overshadowed by Fabius’ and survives only in fragments, cited sparingly by later authors. Cincius’ career and captivity underscore his dual role as participant and chronicler of Rome’s conflict with Carthage.

authors.creator: Qiao Zixin
authors.life: A Roman historian renowned for his work on the Second Punic War, which indicated a shift from annalistic chronicles to focused historical narratives. Coelius was contemporary of C. Gracchus and mentor to the orator L. Crassus, he held no public office but maintained elite connections. His work, Historiae Belli Punici Alterius, emphasized dramatic storytelling with rhetorical speeches and dreams. While his work was partly influenced by Greek sources like Silenus, he critically evaluated them, documenting conflicting versions from both sides. Coelius’ fragmented legacy highlights his role in evolving Roman historiography, and influenced later historians like Livy.

authors.creator: Qiao Zixin
authors.life: A Roman author and statesman. Pliny was born in Comum and later was adopted by his uncle, Pliny the Elder. Rising through Rome’s political ranks, he became consul in AD 100, the curator of Tiber in AD 103 and later governor of Bithynia-Pontus. His fame rests on his Epistulae, offering vivid insights into Roman life, including eyewitness accounts of Vesuvius’ eruption in AD 79, which killed his uncle. His correspondence with Trajan, included in the tenth book of his Epistulae, discussing multiple problems on imperial governance. Combining literary elegance with administrative detail, Pliny’s works show imperial governance, social norms, and personal ethics, cementing his legacy as a bridge between Rome’s intellectual and political worlds.

authors.creator: Qiao Zixin
authors.life: A Roman statesman, military commander, and historian. Born as a member of the prestigious Postumii family, he held key roles in his career: urban praetor, envoy in Greek conflicts, and consul. His consulship was marked by clashes with tribunes over harsh levies for Spain. Later, he joined the commission organizing Achaea as a province, earning him Greek honors. His historical work πραγματική ίστορία mainly focused on military and political events of Roman history. Polybius, biased due to political disputes, criticized his Greek-language history as unskilled. Cicero, however, praised his eloquence and learning. Albinus’ other works, including De aduentu Aeneae, aimed to bridge Roman-Greek culture. His fragmented legacy highlights Rome’s complex engagement with Hellenism.

authors.creator: Qiao Zixin
authors.life: A Roman poet born in Bilbilis, Spain, he later moved to Rome around AD 64, where he spent over three decades navigating literary circles under Flavian emperors. Though financially struggling, he gained patronage from some Roman elites like the wife of Lucan. His 12-book Epigrammata reflects Roman society in the 1st century—exposing urban life with humor, wordplay, and vivid stories. After Domitian’s death, he returned to Spain, enjoying quieter years until his death. Martial’s unique style influenced modern satirists, while his works remain invaluable for insights into 1st-century Rome’s social fabric.

authors.creator: Qiao Zixin
authors.life: A Roman historian and biographer from Gaul, associated with figures like Cicero and Catullus, who dedicated poetry to him. A knight-class scholar, he pioneered Latin biographical literature with De Viris Illustribus , contrasting Greek and Roman leaders with at least 16 books. His surviving works include Lives of Eminent Foreign Commanders, Life of Atticus, and Life of Cato. They blend moralizing anecdotes with historical narrative. Though criticized for factual inaccuracies and reliance on Greek sources like Apollodorus, his accessible style made his texts educational staples. Nepos emphasized cultural parallels, bridging Greek and Roman traditions, influencing later writers like Plutarch.

authors.creator: Qiao Zixin
authors.life: A Roman architect and engineer active during the reign of Caesar and Augustus. Though details of his life remain vague, his work De Architectura, which is the only surviving architectural treatise from antiquity—cements his legacy. Trained in engineering, he served in the Roman military, designing siege engines and aqueducts for the legions. His De Architectura, dedicated to Augustus, systematized classical architecture and set a high standard for later architects, emphasizing three core principles: firmitas (durability), utilitas (utility), and venustas (beauty). Though valued in related studies, it has also been criticized by scholars for its content, which is divorced from actual technology and focuses on formative descriptions. Rediscovered in the Renaissance, his ideas became fundamental to Western architectural practice, bridging ancient wisdom and modern design.

authors.creator: Qiao Zixin
authors.life: A Roman geographer born in Tingentera, Spain. He was active during Claudius’ reign and wrote a book to celebrate Claudius’ invasion of Britain, which is De Chorographia, the earliest surviving Latin geography. Structured in three books, it described the known world to the Romans—Europe, Asia, and Africa—encircled by Oceanus, blending administrative data, Greek scholarship, and mythical elements. Though reliant on Greek sources like Eratosthenes and military reports rather than firsthand exploration, his work preserved geographical knowledge for later authors like Pliny and Strabo and even for medieval Europe. Rediscovered during the Renaissance, its inaccuracies influenced cartography until the Age of Exploration.

authors.creator: Qiao Zixin
authors.life: A Roman agronomist from Gades, Spain, authored De Re Rustica, which is the most systematic agricultural manual in the ancient Roman world. He combined practical estate management experience with Hellenistic knowledge, advocating sustainable practices like crop rotation, soil conservation, and organic fertilization. His work detailed viticulture, livestock breeding and efficient farm administration, emphasizing scientific observation and economic viability. He also promoted slave management, urging incentives over coercion. Columella critiqued former Roman writers by rejecting "land exhaustion" theories. Moreover, he blended the art of poetry and technical prose, preserving Greco-Roman agrarian wisdom, influencing medieval Europe and Enlightenment-era agronomy.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan, Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Lucius Calpurnius Piso Frugi was born between 182 BC and 180 BC. Politically, he was famous for being elected consul in 133 BC and fought in Sicily with some successes, such as taking Morgantina and beginning the attack on the slaves' stronghold at Henna. As a historian, his main literary achievement was The Annales, which comprised at least seven books, and presented a straightforward, annalistic history of Rome, covering early Rome through contemporary events. His work has been singled out for both its chronological centrality in the annalistic tradition and its centrality in many important questions of Roman history and historiography. Later historians like Livy made use of his work. While his death date is uncertain, based on historical context, it is estimated to be around 120 BC.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan; Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Lucius Coelius Antipater was a Roman historian and jurist with no known public office. His lifespan must be deduced from the evidence of the testimonia and fragments, part of which indicated that he was a contemporary of C. Fannius, suggesting that he was alive in the second century BC. His main literary achievement was his seven books on the Second Punic War, which he wrote using not only Roman sources, but also Silenus' Carthaginian account. Later historians like Livy made extensive use of his work. In addition, he was also one of Cicero's main sources for the De Divinatione, particularly on the question of dreams and divine warnings.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan, Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa, born between 64 BC and 62 BC, was a Roman statesman, general and a close friend of Augustus. Literarily, Agrippa wrote an autobiography ( De Vita Sua ) and a geographical commentary that was used later by Strabo and Pliny to construct maps for the empire. Politically, he held several positions including senator after 43 BC and governor of Gaul in 38 BC. He was instrumental in leading Rome to naval victories, which were deemed the primary cause of Mark Antony's defeat at Actium; and was also renowned for overseeing major building projects like the original Pantheon. In 23 BC, a sickly Augustus even handed Agrippa his signet-ring, conferring an unofficial supreme status if Augustus has died. In 12 BC, he went to Pannonia to deal with a revolt but fell ill and died.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Ammianus Marcellinus, a historian of the Roman empire, was born in Syria around 330. He entered into the elite corps of protectores domestici at his youth, then became a personal staff of the general Ursicinus, serving in north Italy, Gaul, Germany, Illyria and Mesopotamia. He shortly withdrew from military activities after Ursicinus' dismission in 359, and reemerged in 363 for Julian's Persian expedition. In his later years, he settled in Rome and began the Res Gestae ( Chronicles of Events), a continuation of Tacitus' History. Marcellinus died around 395.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Velleius Paterculus, a Roman historian, soldier and senator, was born in 20 or 19 BC. He began his career as a military tribune, then joined the staff of Gaius Caesar in eastern provinces in AD 2. During the AD 4-12, he served under the future emperor Tiberius, holding positions of praefectus equitum (AD 4-6) and then quaestor (AD 7). He was designated as praetor in AD 14. His main literary achievement was two volumes of Roman History, usually titled Historiae Romanae (Roman histories), began with Greek mythology and ended in AD 29. The date of his passing is unknown.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Titus Pomponius Atticus, born in 110 BC, from an old-established equestrian family. He earned his cognomen Atticus from his 20-year stay in Athens (86–65 BC). As a lifelong friend of Cicero, he was renowned for his cultural pursuits, business activities and political neutrality. He hardly participated in politics publicly, but exerted influence through his extensive friendships with political elites, such as Caesar, Antony, Octavian. His main literary achievement was Liber Annalis, a chronological table of world. In 33 BC, he committed suicide because of an incurable illness.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Marcus Porcius Cato, known as Cato the Elder, born in 234 BC, was a Roman statesman, orator, and writer. He came from a plebeian family with military tradition, which led him to begin his career in the army during the Second Punic War. As a quaestor in 204 BC, he served under Scipio Africanus in Sicily and Africa. As a consul in 195 BC, he suppressed rebellions in Spain. As a censor in 184 BC, he purged the Senate and opposed luxury. He remained politically active throughout his later years. Cato was a foundational figure in Latin literature. His Origines was the first historical work written in Latin, while his De Agri Cultura remained the oldest surviving Latin prose. As an orator, he was renowned for his numerous speeches and his famous saying “Carthage must be destroyed.” Cato died in 149 BC.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Marcus Terentius Varro, born in 116 BC in a Sabine town, was a scholar, soldier and statesman. He served as praetor and then fought alongside Pompey during the civil war. He received Caesar's pardons but was proscribed by Antony in 43 BC. He survived through political connections and spent his final years in scholarly retirement until his death in 27 BC. Renowned as one of the most prolific Roman writers, he produced nearly 75 works, including De Lingua Latina on Latin linguistics and De Re Rustica on agricultural science.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Apuleius was born around AD 125 at Madaurus in Africa Proconsularis and received his education in Carthage, Athens and Rome. He was a prominent public orator in Carthage and held the chief priesthood of his province. His main literary achievements included the famous novel Metamorphoses (The Golden Ass), philosophical works like De Deo Socratis and De Platone, speeches like Florida and Apologia, and some lost texts on diverse subjects. There is no record of his activities after the 160s, so the year of his passing is unknown.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Marcus Cornelius Fronto, born around AD 95 in Numidia, was a prominent Roman lawyer and orator. He was a suffect consul for two months in 142 and served as a tutor of the future emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, earning imperial favour. In literature, Fronto was renowned for his eloquent correspondence, particularly with Marcus Aurelius, in which he tried to use more expressive and unusual words from early Latin texts. Through these letters, he shared his literary views, criticized Stoicism, and described his personal experience at court. Though only few of his speeches survived, his reputation as a master orator endured. He died around AD 166, likely during the plague.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Decimus Magnus Ausonius, born around AD 310 in Bordeaux, was a Gallo-Roman poet, teacher and statesman. He rose from humble origins to become the tutor of future emperor Gratianus in the mid-360s. He was later honoured with the title comes, the office of quaestor sacri palatii and praetorian prefect, and finally the consulship. His literary works, including the famous poem Mosella, blended classical tradition with personal and regional themes. Though a Christian, he maintained a deep appreciation for Greco-Roman culture. Ausonius passed away after AD 393.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Lactantius, whose full name was Lucius Caelius Firmianus Lactantius, was born around AD 240 in North Africa. He studied under Arnobius and became a prominent Latin rhetorician, later summoned by Emperor Diocletian to teach in Nicomedia. Converted to Christianity, he lost his position during the Great Persecution and dedicated himself to writing apologetic works. His major achievement, Divinae Institutiones, is a seven-book treatise defending Christian doctrine against paganism and philosophy. He also authored De Mortibus Persecutorum, documenting the deaths of emperors who persecuted Christians. In his later years, he tutored Crispus, son of Constantine, and enjoyed the favour of the emperor. Lactantius died around AD 320.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Marcus Annaeus Lucanus, known as Lucan, born in Corduba in Roman Hispania in AD 39, was a prominent Roman poet and nephew of Seneca the Younger. He was renowned for his epic De Bello Civili (also called Pharsalia), which vividly portrayed the civil war between Caesar and Pompey. His work, characterized by its Stoic themes and rhetorical intensity, criticized tyranny and praised republican ideals. Although initially close to Emperor Nero, Lucan later joined the Pisonian conspiracy against Nero, leading to his forced suicide or execution in AD 65.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Aulus Gellius, born between AD 125 and 128, was a Roman scholar and writer. He studied grammar under Sulpicius Apollinaris and rhetoric under Antonius Julianus in Rome. Fronto and philosopher Favorinus profoundly influenced him. He used to be appointed a private law judge in Rome. He also went to Athens, attending lectures by the Platonist Calvenus Taurus, during which he began writing Noctes Atticae, a 20-book collection of notes on diverse topics such as philosophy, history, law and grammar. He continued his writing after returning to Rome and passed away after AD 180.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan
authors.life: Censorinus was a third-century Roman scholar and grammarian, described as doctissimus artis grammaticae (most skilled of the grammatical art) by Priscianus. His extant work, De Die Natali, which he dedicated to his patron Quintus Caerellius in AD 238, explored human life stages and chronological systems, mainly relying on Varro and Suetonius. He also wrote the lost De Accentibus, exploring the relationship between musical theory and grammatical accentuation, an idea probably inspired by Pythagorean mathematics, which he also discussed in De Die Natali. Censorinus' works are valued for its synthesis of science and antiquarianism.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Caius Plinius Secundus was born between 23 and 24 AD, and passed away in 79 AD. His main literary achievement included the Natural History, which described the world and addressed various issues ranging from subjects ranging from astronomy, geography, botany to medicine in 37 volumes. This monumental work has been the foundation of scientific scholarship in Europe for many centuries, and is by far the most detailed ancient source on Roman metallurgy. Politically, he served in Germany with the future emperor Titus, was a commander of the Misenum fleet, and also eventually became a member of the council of Vespasian and Titus. According to his nephew, Pliny the younger, he was known to have been interested in the eruption of Vesuvius and died in the process of trying to further observe the phenomenon.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan and Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Cornelius Sulla, Lucius was born in 138 BC. After serving as a Roman praetor, he marched his army on Rome twice, in 88 and 83/82 BC, which fell to him after the decisive battle of the Colline Gate. As victor in the civil war, there was a general massacre of the Marians, and Sulla was appointed dictator under the Lex Valeria with the title of “Felix.” He was also voted immunity for all his actions, past and future. During this period, he enacted laws to reform the senate, magistracies and lawcourts, while restricting the powers and standing of the tribunes of the plebs. Then, believing in the old prophecy that he did not have long to live, he gradually divested himself of power and retired into private life at the beginning of 79 BC. Literally, he was famous for his Memoirs . He died in 78 BC.

authors.creator: Jin Ziyan and Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Eutropius was a historian whose dates of birth and death were not well-documented – it is only certain that he lived in the fourth century AD. Politically, he was an opponent of Constantine and supporter of Julian and served in Julian’s Persian campaign against the Parthians in AD 363. He was also Valens’ magister memoriae . Literally, he is famous for having published a summary history of Rome, Breviarium ad urbe condita in ten books, with material drawn from Livy. The content of Breviarium covered the foundation of Rome to his own time.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Sempronius Asellio was a Roman historian and soldier whose background remains largely unknown. A testimonium by Aulus Gellius shed light on Sempronius Asellio’s activity in the second century BC. Politically, he was a military tribune at Numantia under Scipio Africanus, holding his junior officer-ship in 134 or 133 BC. Thus, his birthdate is deduced to be around 160 BC. Literally, he wrote an account of his personal experience serving as military tribune. His work reflects the influence of Polybius, suggesting that it was written after Polybius was done. The date of his passing remains unclear.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Fenestella was an antiquarian annalist and historian of the early Imperial period but little is known of his dates or life. Literally, he penned an annalistic historical work on Roman history, or the Annales , in minimally 22 books, spanning the origins of the Roman republic at least to 57 BC. The surviving fragments demonstrate Fenestella’s interest in scholarly and antiquarian questions. His work has been quoted by Pliny the Elder, who also dated Fenestella’s death in the mid-30s AD. However, the date of his passing remains disputed as Jerome also provided evidence of Fenestella’s death in AD 19.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Quintus Lutatius Catulus, born in 150 BC, was a Roman general and consul. He was the stepbrother of Lucius Julius Caesar and Gaius Julius Caesar Strabo. With the help of Marius, he successfully secured consulship in 102 BC. He fought alongside Marius at the Battle of Vercellae against the Cimbri, where they eventually secured a joint victory in 101 BC. Yet, issues with credit-taking for this victory eventually resulted in both turning against each other and becoming bitter enemies. When Marius occupied Rome in 87 BC, rather than going through with the capital charge, Q. Lutatius Catulus committed suicide with his wife. Overall, he was famous for poetry, philosophy and art, and left behind two epigrams, as well as fragments of a memoir De consulatu et de rebus gestis suis about his consulship and his achievements.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Caius Asinius Pollio, born in 76 BC, was a Roman general, politician and historian. He was already a friend of Caesar’s at the Rubicon when civil war broke out in 49 BC. He went on to serve under Curio in Africa, alongside Caesar at Pharsalus and in Africa, thereby taking up various political posts throughout his career. By 39 BC, his campaign against the Albanian Parthini was successful. However, he retired from public life in 38 BC and refused to take sides during the final leg of the civil wars. His own literary work included poetry and oratory, as well as his Historia .

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Valerius Antias was a Roman annalistic historian of the first century whose work unfortunately only survived in fragments. Of the different fragments that remain, Livy mentioned Antias frequently, often in a critical manner, and many regarded his work as typical of non-senatorial historical writing. His first praenomen was not recorded and little is known about his career or social status. Yet there is some consensus of later Roman literary scholarship that Antias lived and wrote before Cicero.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: C. Iulius Hyginus was a Spaniard born between 64 and 60 BC, brought to Rome by Caesar and was a freed slave of Augustus. He received education under Alexander Polyhistor and was both a teacher and friend with Ovid as well as the historian Clodius Licinius. He headed the Palatine Library and wrote on a large range of topics including agriculture, history, geography and astronomy. From Ovid’s work, it could be inferred that Hyginus was alive at least until AD 10, which places his time of death afterwards.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Publius Rutilius Rufus was a Roman statesman and a Stoic who was likely born between 160 and 158 BC. He studied under Panaetius and Scaevola, founders of Roman legal system. He was politically known for having served as a military tribune under Scipio Aemilianus at Numantia and later as legatus in the Jugurthine war. Before becoming consul in 105 BC where he strove to rebuild Rome’s morale, he exchanged accusations and prosecutions with his rival, Aemilius Scaurus. When he was in office, he was known for his uprightness. Yet by 92 BC, Marius’ coalition launched a successful attack on Rutilius and he was falsely convicted of extortion and retired into exile at Smyrna. Here, he wrote his literary works including his autobiography, De Vita Sua , which were heavily quoted by others who came after him. The date of his passing remains unclear.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Quintus Dellius was a Roman diplomat and historian in the first century BC. He was known for his shrewd political career, having switched sides numerous times during the course of the civil wars. He switched allegiance from Dolabella to Cassius, then to Antony and finally to Octavian just before the Battle of Actium in 31 BC. Accordingly, he was labelled by Seneca the Elder in his Suasoriae as the desultor bellorum civilium or “the horse vaulter of the civil wars.” While serving Antony, he was deployed to fetch Cleopatra in Alexandria to a meeting in Tarsus and played an important diplomatic role in orchestrating this historic meeting. His works included an account of Antony’s Parthian campaign, titled the Bellum Parthicum , which was then quoted by later authors like Plutarch. He was also the Dellius to whom Horace addressed Ode 2.3.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Lucius Calpurnius Bibulus was the son of Marcus Calpurnius Bibulus and was born in 68 BC. He pursued higher education in Athens in 45 BC, before joining Brutus in the Civil War. After that, Brutus wrote to Cicero in 43 BC to nominate Calpurnius for a pontificate but was unsuccessful. After Philippi where he was defeated, Calpurnius surrendered to Mark Antony and eventually became part of Antony’s inner circle. He then served as a mediator or diplomatic envoy between him and Octavian, and commanded a squadron of Antony’s fleet. By 35 BC, he was praetor and governed Syria for Antony until 34 to 32 BC where he died.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Gaius Acilius was a Roman senator and historian active in the mid-2nd century BC, particularly around 155 BCE, when he interpreted for Greek philosophers — Carneades, Diogenes, and Critolaus. According to Cicero, he wrote Annales, or a history of Rome, in Greek. He did this until 184 BC. His work, now lost, was cited by later authors including Dionysius of Halicarnassus and Plutarch.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Marcus Aemilius Scaurus was born into a patrician family which had become impoverished in 163 or 162 BC. He considered between engaging in politics or banking before deciding on the former. By 115 BC, Aemilius had acquired consulship and was appointed princeps senatus. He was also known for having been involved in the negotiations following the Jugurthan war in 111 BC, which unfortunately caused him to be suspect of corruption in the Jugurtha issue. Nonetheless, he remained a key figure in Roman politics and by his death, latest in early 88 BC, the age of Marius and Sulla had begun. As such, he was seen as a bridge between the old and new in Roman politics. He also wrote an autobiography, De Vita Sua, in three books.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Lucius Scribonius Libo was born in 90 BC, though some contend that he was born 20 years later (Münzer, 1921). He first appeared in historical record as a moneyer in 63 or 62 BC. Politically, he supported Pompey during the civil wars and was one of his legates. After Pompey’s death, he aligned himself with his son-in-law, Sextus Pompeius, taking a prominent advisory role. He also developed connections with Octavian by agreeing to his sister Scribonia’s marriage to Octavian. In 35 BC, he abandoned Sextus Pompeius for Antony, and was appointed consul in 34 BC. He was still alive in 21 BC and possible died in 13 BC. He left behind his work Annalis.

authors.creator: Jolenta Wong
authors.life: Ctesias was a Greek physician and historian originally from Cnidus, hailing from a long line of doctors. He took part in the Persian expedition in 401 BC, and was then further taken into Artaxerxes where he became the royal physician. As a Persian expert, he wrote works such as Persica , a history of Persia in 23 books. He also wrote Indica , the first Greek book about India which survived mainly in fragments and in the ninth century summary by Photius. However, his reliability as a historian has been questioned by both his contemporaries and modern historians, particularly because his writing seemed to be historically unreliable, possibly due to language issues and the fact that he received information about India second-hand. Regardless, Ctesias’ writing has influenced subsequent authors, such as Diodorus, who seemed to have based his writing on Assyria and Media on the history of Ctesias.

Quintus Claudius Quadrigarius was a Roman historian and lived around the time of Rutilius Rufus and Valerius Antias, during the Sullan period in the first century BC. He was renowned in literature for having written Annales , or Historiae , which consisted of 23 books. He wrote in an annalistic manner and his work was referred to briefly by subsequent historians, including Cicero, Seneca and Gellius.

works.synopsis: Frontinus' De Aquaeductu Urbis Romae is a professional treatise on Rome’s aqueducts and water supply. As Rome’s Curator Aquarum, Frontinus documented the city’s nine aqueducts so far, their construction, history, maintenance, and daily water output. The work combines technical precision and political information. Frontinus prioritized public welfare, advocating fair distribution to fountains, baths, and normal citizens over elite misuse. His empirical approach, rejecting outdated records for firsthand surveys, set new standards for civic engineering. A blend of engineering manual and policy manifesto, it remains a cornerstone for understanding Roman urban planning and water uses.
works.creator: Qiao Zixin

works.synopsis: Frontinus' Strategemata is a tactical manual on classical military stratagems from Greek and Roman history. Organized into four books, it analyzes battlefield cunning, deception, and leadership. Frontinus, drawing on his own campaigns in Britain and Germany, aimed to provide commanders with practical tools rather than abstract theory. He emphasized adaptability, psychological warfare, and creativity, illustrating how intelligence and creativity often outweighed pure violence. He not only documented Roman discipline, but also acknowledged lessons from adversaries like Hannibal. Blending historical analysis with doable advice, the Strategemata became a fundamental text for later military theorists and generals.
works.creator: Qiao Zixin

works.synopsis: Ovid's Fasti is an unfinished elegiac poem exploring the Roman calendar, blending myths, religion, and politics. Planned in 12 books (one per month), only the first six survive. Each book details festivals, rituals, and star movements, offering explanations for traditions like the Lupercalia or Matronalia. Ovid interweaves divine myths with historical events, glorifying Emperor Augustus to align imperial ideology with ancient rites. Despite its incompleteness due to Ovid’s exile, the work remains vital for understanding Roman religion, societal values, daily life and the interplay of time, memory, and power.
works.creator: Qiao Zixin

works.synopsis: Pliny’s Epistulae comprise 10 volumes of his letters, blending personal reflection, political insight, and historical documentation. The letters depict Roman elite life, legal affairs, and imperial governance. The most famous entries describe the eruption of Vesuvius, chronicling his uncle Pliny the Elder’s death. Book 10, featuring exchanges with Trajan, reveals administrative challenges in Bithynia-Pontus, including early Christian trials. Pliny’s vivid accounts of villas, social customs, and moral dilemmas offer colorful pictures of the Roman world in the first century. Stylistically, his letters balance rhetoric with intimacy, influencing Renaissance humanists like Erasmus.
works.creator: Qiao Zixin

works.synopsis: Pliny’s Panegyricus is a formal oration praising Emperor Trajan, delivered during Pliny’s consulship. Expanded into a literary text, it contrasts Trajan’s “ideal rule”—modesty, justice, and civic engagement—with the tyranny of his predecessor Domitian. Blending flattery with political ideology, Pliny highlights Trajan’s accessibility, military restraint, and respect for the Senate, framing him as a “citizen-emperor” who restores Republican virtues. While the work was criticized as sycophantic, it reflects the era’s shift from autocracy to collaborative governance. As the earliest surviving Roman imperial panegyric, it sets a template for later encomia, influencing Renaissance writers.
works.creator: Qiao Zixin

works.synopsis: A 12-book collection, revolutionized Latin satire with over 1,500 poems. Combining humor and critique, Epigrammata dissect Imperial Rome’s social hierarchy, mocking hypocritical elites, and urban chaos. His verses blend biting sarcasm with playful wordplay, often ending in ironic twists that expose moral decay. However, Martial also celebrates friendship, simple joys, and observations of daily life—from banquets to street scenes. Praised for brevity and vivid imagery, his epigrams pioneered the genre, influencing modern writers like G. E. Lessing and Ben Johnson.
works.creator: Qiao Zixin

works.synopsis: Cornelius Nepos' De Viris Illustribus, was a pioneering biographical anthology contrasting Greek and Roman leaders. Organized by profession (generals, historians, poets), it originally spanned over 16 books, but now only Lives of Eminent Foreign Commanders and some fragments of Roman biographies survive. Emphasizing moral lessons over historical rigor, Nepos blended anecdotes, cultural comparisons, and panegyric to bridge Greek and Roman traditions. While criticized for factual errors, plain style and reliance on Greek sources like Apollodorus, it still remains a key educational text. The work influenced Plutarch’s Parallel Lives and marked a foundational step in Latin biographical literature.
works.creator: Qiao Zixin

works.synopsis: Vitruvius’ De Architectura, is the only surviving architectural treatise from antiquity. Comprising ten volumes, it systematically outlines principles of architecture, engineering, and urban planning. Vitruvius blends Greek theory with Roman innovation, detailing materials like ancient concrete, classical pillars (Doric, Ionic, Corinthian), theater, aqueduct engineering, and siege machinery. He links architecture to human anatomy, inspiring later architects like Leonardo da Vinci. Dedicated to Emperor Augustus, the work emphasizes harmony between nature and design. But its content has been criticized by many scholars for theoretical description rather than actual technology, makes people wonder whether ancient architects followed the guidance of his book. Rediscovered in the Renaissance, the De Architectura inspired many western architects and engineers.
works.creator: Qiao Zixin

works.synopsis: Pomponius Mela's De Chorographia is the earliest surviving geography treatise in Latin. Organized in three books, it outlines the known world to the Romans—Europe, Asia, and Africa—surrounded by the ocean Oceanus. Pomponius blends Greek scholarship like Eratosthenes’ climate zones with Roman official documents, describing regions from Spain to India. He also documents myths while describing continents far away, leaving inaccuracies in his work. Though reliant on secondhand sources and legends, his work reflects Roman imperial perspectives, emphasizing trade routes and provincial boundaries. Rediscovered in the Renaissance, its errors influenced medieval cartography.
works.creator: Qiao Zixin

works.synopsis: Columella's De Re Rustica, a 12-volume agricultural treatise, stands as ancient Rome’s most systematic agricultural manual. Blending technical prose with literary beauty, it covers soil science, crop rotation, viticulture, arboriculture, livestock breeding, beekeeping, and estate management. Book 10 uniquely employs hexameter verse to discuss horticulture, honoring Virgil’s Georgics. Columella emphasizes sustainability, advocating green manure, composting, and soil conservation to counter degradation. He critiques earlier agrarian theory of “land exhaustion”. Detailed advice on irrigation, slave labor incentives, and profit-driven farming reflects his pragmatic idealism. Integrating Greek agronomy with Roman practices, the work preserved classical knowledge through medieval Europe, later inspiring Enlightenment agronomists.
works.creator: Qiao Zixin

works.synopsis: The Roman emperor Augustus once wrote a thirteen-book autobiographical work, titled De Vita Sua (On his Own Life), which was much longer than the extant Res Gestae. Only a few fragments survive now (Smith and Powell 2009, ix). This autobiography was written in Latin and chronicled Augustus' life, ending with the Cantabrian War (29–19 B.C.) in Spain (Suet. Aug., 85.1). The text primarily focused on his military achievements and the divine favour for his career, similar in style to Sulla's autobiography (Smith and Powell 2009, x–xii).
works.creator: Jin Ziyan
works.quoting_information: Pliny Nat.; Serv. On Ecl.; Serv. On Aen.; Plut. Brut.; Plut. Ant.; Plut. Comp. Dem. Cic.; Ulpian, Dig.; Tert. De anim.; App. B. Civ.; App. Ill.; Suet. Aug.

works.synopsis: Caius Sallustius Crispus,often known simply as Sallust, was a renowned Roman historian and politician, writing his major work Historiae(Histories) during the period of the Second Triumvirate. Histories covered the years 78 BC to 67 BC, the turbulent years after the death of the dictator Sulla, providing context for the chaos that eventually led to Julius Caesar's rise and the Triumvirate period. While two of Sallust's works Bellum Catilinae and Bellum Iugurthinum survived intact, Histories unfortunately did not survive intact and was preserved in about 500 fragments, being collected in manuscripts during the middle ages.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan; Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Serv. Commentary on Virgil; Non. De compendiosa doctrina.

works.synopsis: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa was a famous Roman statesman and general who finished his autobiography De Vita Sua around the same time as Augustus'. De Vita Sua did not survive intact as a whole and consisted of at least two books. Three fragments remain, two of which were preserved by Pliny the Elder, and one in the commentary of the Georgics by Servius. The contents of the autobiography included the events of his life up to his aedileship in 33 BC in the second book. Since the rest of the books did not survive, it is deduced that it would almost certainly have included accounts of Agrippa's extensive public works in the city of Rome, which are among his lasting achievements.
works.work_title: Vipsanius Agrippa, M. De Vita Sua
works.creator: Jin Ziyan, Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Serv. Commentary on the Georgics of Vergil; Plin. HN

works.synopsis: Res Gestae ( Chronicles of Events) was written by Ammianus Marcellinus during his settlement in Rome after 380, and was published serially during 390s. It was a 31-book Latin historical work, originally covered Roman history from Emperor Nerva's accession in 96 to Valens' death at the Battle of Adrianople in 378. Only books 14-31, about the period 353 to 378, survived and transmitted through a single 9th-century manuscript. Res Gestae combined the style of Tacitus' annalistic history and the style of imperial biographies in author's time, was renowned for its vivid character sketches.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan

works.synopsis: Velleius Paterculus wrote two-book Historiae Romanae (Roman Histories) in Latin and dedicated it to the consul Marcus Vinicius in AD 30. It was a summary of Roman history from the fall of Troy to AD 29. The first book (mostly lost) ended with the destruction of Carthage in 146 BC, while the second book mainly focused on the deeds of Caesar, Augustus and Tiberius. Although Paterculus' pro-Tiberian perspective long drew criticism, his work was valuable as the only surviving Latin historical work between Livy and Tacitus.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan

works.synopsis: The Liber Annalis (Yearly Accounts), a chronological table of world centered on Rome, was composed in Latin by Titus Pomponius Atticus. It gave an account of the yearly magistrates and most important events from the founding of Rome to the mid-1st century BC, emphasizing precise dating and genealogical records. Atticus wrote his chronicle in a concise style, rather than lengthy historical discourses, reflecting his blend of Greek and Roman literary styles. The text survives only in fragments, preserved through citations by later authors including Cicero, Nepos, Asconius, and Solinus.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan
works.quoting_information: Solin. Collectanea Rerum Memorabilium; Asc. Corn.; Asc. Pis.; Cic. Brut.; Cic. Att.; Nep. Vita Hannibalis

works.synopsis: De Agri Cultura(On Agriculture), a guide to agriculture and the earliest surviving prose work in Latin, was written by Marcus Porcius Cato at an unspecified date, probably in his later years. This work, of moderate length, composed with concise and practical instructions, while lacking of systematic organization and containing repetitive sections. Primarily focusing on grape and olive cultivation,it also briefly mentioned horticulture, medicinal treatments and recipes, offering valuable insights into Roman agriculture and society. This remains Cato's only complete surviving work.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan

works.synopsis: De Lingua Latina (On the Latin Language) is a grammatical and linguistic work by the Roman scholar Marcus Terentius Varro. According to the correspondences between Cicero and Atticus, De Lingua Latina was composed between 47 and 43 BC and ultimately dedicated to Cicero. It discussed about etymology, morphology, grammar and usage of Latin language across 25 books, though only Books 5-10 survive intact. The remaining books are fragments, preserved through quotations by later grammarians in manuscripts.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan

works.synopsis: Antiquitates Rerum Divinarum (Antiquities of Divine Matters) by Marcus Terentius Varro is a 16-book antiquarian work on ancient Roman religious institutions, dedicated to Caesar as Pontifex Maximus. It might be published together with 25-book Antiquitates Rerum Humanarum in the late 50s BC, or after Humanarum during the period of Caesar's dictatorship. This work systematically explored cults, priesthoods, and rituals in ancient Rome, restoring to the Romans their lost cultural identity. The original book was lost, surviving only in fragments cited mainly by Augustine in his De Civitate Dei.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan
works.quoting_information: August.De civ. D.; Plin.HN; Serv.Commentary on Virgil; Macrob.Sat.; Festus,Gloss. Lat.; Lydus,Mens.; Gell.NA; Non. De Compendiosa Doctrina; Lactant.Div. inst.; Isid.Etym.; Tert.Ad nat.; Tert.De anim..

works.synopsis: The Origines(Origins) was composed by the Roman statesman Marcus Porcius Cato in his later years, likely in the 150s BC. This work, considered the first historical Latin prose, comprised of seven books and divided into two main parts: the first three books focused on the origins of Rome and other Italian peoples, while the remaining four books covered Rome's wars, including the Punic Wars and events up to 149 BC. In the text, Cato omitted the names of military commanders, emphasizing the collective achievements of the populus Romanus. The Origins survived only in fragments, cited by later authors, such as Cicero, Lydus and Servius.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan
works.quoting_information: Cic. Planc.; Lydus, Mag.; Just. praef.; Serv. On Aen.; Hyg. Fab.; Myth. Vat.; Origo Gentis Romanae; Euseb. Chron.; Festus, Gloss. Lat.; Ter. Phorm; Gell. NA; Charisius, Gramm.

works.synopsis: Metamorphoses (commonly known as The Golden Ass), composed by the Roman author Apuleius around AD 150-180, was the only complete surviving Latin novel from antiquity. The story followed Lucius, a man accidentally transformed into a donkey because of a magical mishap, and underwent a series of misadventures before being saved by the goddess Isis. The novel blended elements of comedy, satire and religious themes, concluding with Lucius' spiritual awakening and conversion to Isis, which might reflect Apuleius' own involvement in some mystery cults, as attested in his another work Apologia.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan

works.synopsis: De Mortibus Persecutorum (On the Deaths of the Persecutors) is a historical and Christian apologetic work by Lactantius, written around AD 313-314. It chronicles the downfall of Roman emperors who persecuted Christians, describing their deaths as divine punishments. At the beginning, it briefly covers early persecutors (Nero to Aurelius); then it details the Great Persecution under Diocletian Tetrarchy, focusing on the gruesome fates of Galerius and Maximinus Daia; at last, it concludes with Christianity's triumph under Constantine. The text survives intact in a single 11th-century manuscript.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan

works.synopsis: Divinae Institutiones (Divine Institutes), is a Christian apologetic work by Lactantius, written between AD 305 and 313 during the Diocletian persecution, with some dedications to Emperor Constantine added around 321 to 323. This work systematically refutes pagan religions and philosophies while presenting Christianity as the synthesis of sapientia (true wisdom) and religio (moral obligation). Structured in seven books, it covers topics like the falsity of pagan gods, the errors of philosophers, the theology of Christ, justice and eschatology. Notably, it blends classical authorities (Cicero, Vergil, Ovid and other writers) with Scripture. The text survives fully in medieval manuscripts.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan

works.synopsis: Noctes Atticae (Attic Nights) is a miscellany, written by Roman scholar Aulus Gellius and finished around AD 180. Its title refers to Gellius' winter nights in Athens where he began drafting notes. Organized into twenty books of loosely connected chapters, the work covers grammar, philosophy, history and law through anecdotes, quotations (including numerous fragments of lost texts), and scholarly discussions with his teachers such as Favorinus and Calvenus Taurus. While Book 8 is lost, the text remains largely intact, offering insights into second-century Roman intellectual life.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan

works.synopsis: De Die Natali (On the Birthday), written by the Roman scholar Censorinus, is a treatise dedicated to his patron Quintus Caerellius to celebrate Caerellius' 49th birthday in the summer of AD 238. The work blends antiquarian scholarship and philosophical inquiry, and is divided into two parts: the first part (Chapters 1-15) explores human life, birth, and biological generation, while the second part (Chapters 16-24) examines chronological systems and divisions of time. Notably, Chapter 21 preserves the Varronian chronology, which dates events ab urbe condita (from Rome's foundation), providing unique details about Roman eras and festivals. The text survives in a single 8th-century manuscript, but breaks off mid-sentence in Chapter 24.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan

works.synopsis: Posidonius was a polymath who wrote on many topics, including Historia (or Histories ), covering Roman history after 155 BC and described Rome’s dominance over the Hellenistic world. Posidonius likely referred to sources such as Rutilius Rufus’ history of Rome in the composition of his work during his lifetime. While his complete Histories did not survive, many fragments have been preserved in quotations by different authors, including Athenaeus, Plutarch and Diodorus.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan and Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Ath.Deipnosophistae; Str.Geog.; Plut.Vit.; Diog. Laert.Vitae Philosophorum; Sen.Ep..

works.synopsis: Cornelius Sulla, Lucius was a pivotal figure in Roman political history and according to Plutarch, he worked on his Memoirs until the end of his life in 78 BC. His work was arranged as a chronological narrative, and Book 22 was left incomplete, eventually to be finished by his freedman Epicadus. The beginning of his memories revolved around Sulla’s ancestry and relationships, including a dedication to Lucullus, Sulla’s friend and companion in the Mithridatic Wars. The bulk of the work was military in nature and full of omens and portents, by reference to the favour of the gods. Yet, by accident or design, Sulla’s Memoirs did not leave a justification of his political actions during his dictatorship at Rome in his autobiography.
works.creator: Jolenta Wong and Jin Ziyan
works.quoting_information: Ath.Deipnosophistae; Gell. NA; Plut. Sull.; Plut. Mar.; Plut. Luc.; Plin. HN; Cic.Div.; Plut. Mor..

works.synopsis: As a historian, Eutropius is famous for having published Breviarium ad urbe condita in ten books, covering the foundation of Rome to his own time. The Breviarium ended with the brief reign of Jovian, which suggested that Eutropius wrote this during the reign of the emperors Valentinian I (reigned between AD 364 and AD 375) and Valens (reigned between AD 364 and AD 378). While the Breviarium was short and did not suggest particularly deep historical or geographical expertise on Eutropius’ part, it was well-balanced and demonstrated good judgement and impartiality. In keeping with the brevity of the work, Eutropius focused mainly on the most important events. The popularity of his work was demonstrated through the many later historians, including Jerome, who cited him as a source.
works.creator: Jin Ziyan and Jolenta Wong

works.synopsis: Sempronius Asellio was a Roman historian whose works were unfortunately not preserved in full. There remain around fifteen fragments, and the title of his work may have been Res Gestae , Historia or Historiae . His work reflected the influence of Polybius, suggesting that his work began in 146 BC, written after Polybius’ was completed. While he wrote contemporary history, he also believed that history should be educational in terms of morality. As such, he was not only concerned with penning methodical history, but also in understanding the causes of events, explaining motives and reasons, thereby establishing a different style of writing history.
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Gell. ; Prisc. GL ; Non. ; Charisius; Serv. Aen. ; Verg. G.

works.synopsis: As an annalistic historian, Fenestella is famous for having published his Annales , an annalistic historical work on Roman history, in at least 22 books. Based on Pliny the Elder’s and Jerome’s accounts, it can be deduced that Fenestella wrote his Annales during the late Augustus period as well as Tiberius’ reign. The Annales covered the events from the origins of the Roman republic to at least 57 BC. From existing fragments, it can be deduced that Fenestella included historical information such as the life of Terrence and conflicts between Marius and Sulla. Some of Fenestella’s other work were also quoted by Pliny the Elder in his work Naturalis historia .
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Non. ; Tert. Adv. Valent. ; Plut. Mor. ; Ulpian Dig. ; Sen. Ep. ; Lydus. Mag ; Suet. Vita Ter. ; Frontin; Macrob; Plut. Vit. Sull

works.synopsis: Quintus Lutatius Catulus, born in 150 BC, was a Roman general and consul and a man of culture, with varied interests in philosophy, art and poetry. He wrote light verse and, according to Cicero, a memoir De consulatu et de rebus gestis suis ( On his consulship and achievements ) about his career, which unfortunately was not preserved in full. The remaining fragments as quoted by Plutarch in Mar. are unassigned, but probably from the De consulate . They record Lutatius’ experience while fighting with Marius. Therefore, given the title and fragments’ contents, it must have been written after the securement of his consulship in 102 BC.
works.work_title: Lutatius Catulus, Q., De consufatu et de rebus gestis suis
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Plut. Mar. ; Varro. Ling.

works.synopsis: Caius Asinius Pollio was famous for his literary work in various genres, including poetry, speeches and his Historia , or History . It was inferred that he did not actively embark on History until Sallust’s death in 35 or 34 BC, of which some parts may have been written in the early years of Augustus’ principate, and included contents such as Pompey’s death. While History was not preserved in full, fragments remained in the works of Plutarch and Appian, who mainly quoted Asinius’ work for the Civil War. In addition, Livy also seemed to have included material from History into his own account. Moreover, in his reflections on the death of Priam in Aen , Virgil also seemed to have been influenced by the accounts of Asinius Pollio Caius.
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Val. Max.; Strabo; Suet. Iul.; Plut. Caes.; Plut. Pomp.; App. B. Civ.; Joseph. AJ; Sen. Suas.; Prisc. GL;

works.synopsis: Valerius Antias was a Roman annalistic historian of the first century. Notably, his work, often cited as Historia , Historiae or annales , comprised at least 75 books and covered the history of Rome from the origins down to at least 91 BC. His first book extended to the birth of Romulus, and his work most likely extended at least to the death of Sulla. Often considered a “Sullan annalist,” there is much consensus that Antias wrote during and following Sulla's dictatorship, between 85 BC and 60 BC. While his work unfortunately did not survive in full, he had been heavily referenced in 65 surviving fragments, including 33 from Livy. While Livy has quoted Antias extensively, he had also heavily criticised Antias of exaggerating numbers and inventing freely. Yet, Livy’s contemporary Dionysius of Halicarnassus named Antias as one of the approved Roman writers of history.
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Vell.; Asc. Corn. ; Plin. HN ; Fronto.; Charisius; Gell.; Plut. Rom. ; DH; Macrob.; Arn.; Livy;

works.synopsis: A freed slave of Augustus and the head of the Palatine Library, C. Iulius Hyginus was born in the first century BC. His writings, now lost, covered a wide range of topics including agriculture, history, geography and astronomy. He was not known as a historian but rather more for his expertise in language and literature. Yet, of his historical or antiquarian writings, his works De vita rebusque inlustrium virorum or De illustribus viris was one of two titles that has remained known. The De illustribus viris was written in at least six books and was quoted in Aulus Gellius and Asconius. According to Gellius, Hyginus included not only literary and culture figures in his work but also military and political ones. However, it remains uncertain whether both Greek and Roman figures were included.
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Ascon. Pis. ; Gell.

works.synopsis: Publius Rutilius Rufus was a Roman statesman who had a successful political career prior to being convicted of extortion and retired into exile at Smyrna between 88 and 85 BC. Here, he wrote his literary works, including the De Vita Sua , an autobiography of his life in at least five books. While it did not survive in full, fragments remain as his work was quoted by those after him, including Charisus and Diomedes. He wrote the De Vita Sua in Latin and in first-person narrative, penning mostly the events of his life, the exception being the mention of the death of Scipio Africanus which happened before his time.
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Charisius.; Isid. Etym.; Diom. Grammatici Latini ; Plut. Pomp

works.synopsis: Quintus Dellius was a shrewd Roman diplomat in the first century BC, an addressee of Ode 2.3 by Horace and a historian whose work included the Bellum Parthicum , which was later quoted by Plutarch later in his Life of Antony . The Bellum Parthicum was a first-hand narrative of Antony’s Parthian campaign which did not survive in full. Fragments reveal that this work might have been instrumental in contributing to the detailed extant accounts of the campaign today. It was likely that the work was written in Latin.
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Strabo; Plut. Ant. ;

works.synopsis: Lucius Calpurnius Bibulus wrote a work, whose title was likely Plutarch’s own, titled ἀπομνημονεύματα, or reminiscences. Based on Plutarch’s use of this title, and with reference to how other authors employed the term, it seems likely that this was a collection of Brutus’ sayings. Yet, there are fragments that suggest that it might have comprised more general reflections by Brutus.
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Plut. Brut.

works.synopsis: Gaius Acilius was a Roman senator and historian and was known for having written Historia or Annales, a history of Rome in Greek, which appeared in 142 BC. It unfortunately did not survive in full. The few fragments of his work that remain suggest that Acilius wrote in the style of older Roman annalists, and that his work covered the history from early Italian times to 184 BC. Given that it was written in Greek, his work would have made Roman achievements intelligible to Greek audiences, and was later reproduced in Latin by Claudius Quadrigarius.
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Plut. Rom; Cic. Off.; Livy; Dion. Hal.; Paradoxographus Vaticanus

works.synopsis: Marcus Aemilius Scaurus was a famous politician who was appointed consul and princeps senatus by 115 BC. He wrote an autobiography, De Vita Sua, dedicated to L. Fufidius in three books. It was a complete account of his life to the time of writing, but the exact period of work composition remains unknown. Based on two of the fragments available, the De Vita Sua seemed to deviated from the norm and was written in first-person account. While Aemilius was not particularly famous, as shared by Cicero, his innovative writing style was indicative of a landmark shift in writing in Latin literature. Aemilius’ work was quoted by authors such as Valerius Maximus who came after him.
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Val. Max.; Charisius Gramm.; Diomedes GL

works.synopsis: Lucius Scribonius Libo was a well-established political figure who had connections to both Octavian and Pompey. He was also a friend of Cicero and Marius, and wrote a work called the Annalis that aimed to detail different dates and careers. In preparing for his own work, Cicero used Scribonius’ for purposes of cross-referencing dates in 45 BC, suggesting that the Annalis must have been completed before that. In addition, since Cicero planned for his work to be set in 146 BC, it can be concluded that Scribonius’ Annalis covered this period. There remain few other details about Scribonius’ literary contributions.
works.creator: Jolenta Wong
works.quoting_information: Cic. Att.
